metadata.archetype_justification: Zappa is a CLI tool and Lambda runtime library with no persistent data store, no HTTP API surface of its own, no authentication enforcement surface, and no write operations.
metadata.context: Python framework for deploying WSGI apps on AWS Lambda.

findings.title: No HTTP/RPC surface — CLI tool only
findings.description: Zappa exposes no HTTP/RPC surface of its own. It is a CLI tool (zappa deploy, zappa update, zappa tail) that creates API Gateway resources for user applications. The tool itself has no API that an agent would call directly.
findings.gap: No API surface exists to document — this is expected for a CLI/library tool.
findings.recommendation: If agent integration is desired, consider wrapping Zappa CLI commands in a task-based API or using AWS SDK calls directly.

findings.title: No HTTP/RPC surface — machine-readable spec not applicable
findings.description: No HTTP/RPC surface — machine-readable spec is not applicable. Zappa is a CLI tool; its interface is command-line arguments, not an API spec.
findings.recommendation: None needed. The zappa template command outputs CloudFormation JSON for deployed API Gateway resources.

findings.title: No HTTP/RPC surface — structured errors not applicable
findings.description: No HTTP/RPC surface — structured error responses are not applicable. The Lambda handler returns structured JSON error responses for user applications as pass-through behavior.

findings.title: No write API endpoints — CLI tool
findings.description: Agent scope is read-only. Additionally, Zappa has no write API endpoints — it is a CLI tool. Write operations are user-initiated CLI commands, not agent-callable endpoints.

ara_metadata.conditional_resolution: Resolved to INFO: agent_scope=read-only AND no write API endpoints exist.
ara_metadata.resolution_reasoning: Read-only agents do not execute write operations, and Zappa has no write API endpoints to call — it is a CLI tool.

findings.title: CLI text output — no agent-consumable responses
findings.description: Zappa's Lambda handler returns JSON responses to API Gateway for user applications. The CLI itself outputs text to stdout. No agent-consumable API responses exist from Zappa itself.

findings.title: Async support for user apps only — no agent surface
findings.description: Zappa supports async task execution via Lambda/SNS for user applications. As a CLI tool, Zappa's own operations are synchronous. No agent-callable async patterns exist.

findings.title: Event sources for user apps — no Zappa events
findings.description: Zappa supports SNS, SQS, DynamoDB Streams, Kinesis, and EventBridge as event sources for user applications. Zappa itself does not emit events for its own state changes.
findings.gap: CLI tool state changes are not event-emitting.

findings.title: No API surface — rate limit headers not applicable
findings.description: Zappa creates API Gateway usage plans and throttling for user applications. It has no API surface of its own that would require rate limit headers.

findings.title: Delegates authentication to AWS IAM via boto3
findings.description: Zappa delegates all authentication to AWS IAM via boto3 sessions. It does not implement or enforce machine identity authentication itself. Deployed Lambda functions assume IAM roles defined by assume_policy.json.
findings.gap: System does not issue or enforce agent identities — authentication is a consumer/platform responsibility.
findings.recommendation: Agent identity for Zappa-deployed applications should be configured at the API Gateway/Lambda level, not within Zappa itself.

findings.title: Default IAM policies are broad but overridable
findings.description: Zappa provides IAM policy templates with broad wildcards. These are defaults for convenience — users can override with scoped custom policies. Zappa does not enforce permissions itself.
findings.gap: Default policies are overly broad, but documented and overridable. Zappa is a tool, not a target system.
findings.recommendation: Document best practices for least-privilege policies when deploying agent-facing applications via Zappa.

findings.title: Action-level auth delegated to AWS IAM
findings.description: Zappa does not implement action-level authorization. It delegates to AWS IAM which natively supports action-level controls.
findings.gap: System does not enforce action-level auth — consumer/platform responsibility.

findings.title: Identity propagation via Lambda event/context pass-through
findings.description: Zappa's Lambda handler passes through requestContext (authorizer context, Cognito identity, IAM caller identity) to user WSGI/ASGI applications. Identity propagation is handled by API Gateway and Lambda, not by Zappa code.
findings.gap: Pass-through behavior — no identity propagation logic in Zappa itself.

findings.title: Credentials via boto3 chain and S3 REMOTE_ENV
findings.description: Zappa supports loading secrets from S3 via REMOTE_ENV setting. No hardcoded credentials found. AWS credentials consumed from standard boto3 credential chain.
findings.gap: No formal secrets rotation mechanism — S3-based secrets require manual rotation.
findings.recommendation: Consider recommending AWS Secrets Manager integration over S3-based REMOTE_ENV for automatic rotation.

findings.title: No audit logging — consumer responsibility
findings.description: Zappa does not implement audit logging. It relies on AWS CloudTrail for API calls and CloudWatch Logs for application logging. No CloudTrail configuration is managed by Zappa itself.
findings.gap: Audit logging is a platform/consumer concern for this tool.
findings.recommendation: User applications deployed via Zappa should configure CloudTrail and structured logging independently.

ara_metadata.conditional_resolution: Resolved to INFO: has_auth_surface=false AND has_write_operations=false. System does not execute agent-invoked write operations — audit logging is a consumer responsibility.
ara_metadata.resolution_reasoning: Zappa is a CLI tool/library that does not execute agent-invoked operations. Audit logging is owned by the consuming applications and the AWS platform (CloudTrail).

findings.title: Agent identity suspension — consumer responsibility
findings.description: System does not issue or enforce agent identities — suspension is a consumer responsibility. Agent identity suspension for Zappa-deployed apps would be handled via IAM role deactivation or API Gateway API key deletion.
findings.gap: Zappa does not manage agent identities.

findings.title: CLI rollback command — no agent-callable compensation
findings.description: Zappa's CLI supports 'zappa rollback <n>' to revert to previous Lambda versions. This is a user-initiated CLI operation, not an agent-callable API. The tool has no multi-step write workflows requiring compensation.

ara_metadata.conditional_resolution: Resolved to INFO: agent_scope=read-only AND stateless-utility archetype with no write operations. Stateless utilities have no multi-step write sequences.
ara_metadata.resolution_reasoning: Zappa is a stateless CLI tool with no agent-callable write workflows. The rollback command is user-initiated, not agent-invoked.

findings.title: CLI status command — no agent-queryable state
findings.description: Zappa's CLI provides 'zappa status' to query deployment state. This is a CLI command, not a queryable API. The Lambda handler itself is stateless.
findings.gap: CLI tool with no agent-queryable state surface.

findings.title: Stateless — no concurrent write concerns
findings.description: Zappa is stateless. No concurrent write operations exist. Lambda concurrency is managed at the AWS platform level.

findings.title: No circuit breakers — CLI tool uses boto3 defaults
findings.description: Zappa does not implement circuit breakers or retry logic. Its Lambda handler directly invokes user WSGI/ASGI apps. The CLI uses boto3's built-in retry logic.
findings.gap: CLI tool without runtime resilience requirements.
findings.recommendation: User applications should implement their own resilience patterns.

findings.title: No HTTP/RPC surface — rate limiting not applicable
findings.description: System exposes no HTTP/RPC surface — API-layer rate limiting is not applicable. Zappa creates API Gateway throttling for user applications but has no API surface of its own.

findings.title: No write operations — transaction limits not applicable
findings.description: Read-only agents cannot modify records, trigger spend, or delete data. Zappa has no transaction limit mechanism because it has no agent-callable write operations.

findings.title: Lambda auto-scaling — no capacity concerns for CLI tool
findings.description: Zappa-deployed Lambda functions auto-scale via AWS Lambda's concurrency model. Zappa does not own or manage infrastructure capacity.
findings.gap: CLI tool with no runtime capacity concerns.

findings.title: No draft/pending state — CLI tool
findings.description: Zappa has no draft/pending state mechanism. Commands either execute immediately or are dry-run capable. No agent-callable approval workflow exists.
findings.gap: CLI tool with no agent-callable state changes.

findings.title: No configurable approval gates — CLI tool
findings.description: No configurable approval gates exist within Zappa. The CD workflow uses GitHub environment protection rules for Zappa's own releases, not for user applications.

findings.title: Staging is consumer concern — test suite uses mocks
findings.description: Library/utility — staging environments are consumer concerns. Zappa supports multi-stage deployments for user apps. Zappa itself uses mocked AWS calls (placebo) for testing.

findings.title: Not a data-handling target — no sensitive data
findings.description: Stage A = No. Zappa does not store, process, or transmit sensitive data. It handles deployment artifacts temporarily in S3 and passes environment variables to Lambda, but does not own or persist user data.

ara_metadata.conditional_resolution: Resolved to INFO: Stage A = No — not a data-handling target. Dev-library-application override also applies.
ara_metadata.resolution_reasoning: Zappa is a deployment tool that does not store, process, or log PII/PHI/financial/credential data. It temporarily handles deployment zips in S3 and passes env vars to Lambda at cold start.

findings.title: No persistent data — residency not applicable
findings.description: No persistent data store and no user-data logging — residency requirements do not apply. Zappa deploys to user-specified AWS regions.
findings.gap: No data residency concern for the tool.

ara_metadata.conditional_resolution: Resolved to INFO: has_persistent_data_store=false AND has_logging_of_user_data=false. No persistent data store and no user-data logging.
ara_metadata.resolution_reasoning: Zappa holds no data subject to residency constraints. It deploys to user-specified regions but does not persist user data.

findings.title: No query API — CLI tool
findings.description: Zappa has no query API. It is a CLI tool. The Lambda handler passes requests through to user applications.

findings.title: No persistent data — no system of record concern
findings.description: Zappa owns no persistent data. Deployment state is stored in AWS (CloudFormation stacks, Lambda versions, S3 buckets) — AWS is the system of record.

findings.title: Stateless utility — no temporal data concerns
findings.description: Zappa is stateless and does not manage temporal data. Downgraded to INFO for stateless-utility archetype.

findings.title: No user data in logs — PII risk not applicable
findings.description: System does not log user data and holds no user data — PII-in-logs risk is not applicable. Common Log Format logs method, path, status, and size only.
findings.gap: No PII in Zappa's logging surface.
findings.recommendation: None needed. Note: DEBUG mode logs raw Lambda events which could contain user request data.

findings.title: No datasets — data quality not applicable
findings.description: Zappa owns no datasets. No data quality metrics exist because there is no data to measure.

findings.title: Semantic versioning via PyPI — no formal API contracts
findings.description: Zappa is published to PyPI with semantic versioning (v0.62.1). CHANGELOG.md documents breaking changes. Comprehensive test suite (8,400+ lines) serves as implicit contract validation.
findings.gap: No formal contract testing for CLI interface stability.
findings.recommendation: Consider documenting CLI interface stability guarantees if agents will wrap CLI commands.

findings.title: Clear configuration field names
findings.description: Zappa's configuration keys use clear, descriptive names (app_function, aws_region, s3_bucket, django_settings, keep_warm, timeout_seconds). No legacy abbreviations.

findings.title: README as primary documentation — no data catalog needed
findings.description: No data catalog exists — expected for a CLI tool/library with no data. README serves as primary documentation for configuration options.

findings.title: X-Ray support for user apps — library tracing is consumer concern
findings.description: Library/utility — tracing and correlation are consumer concerns. Zappa supports X-Ray tracing as pass-through for deployed applications. Uses standard Python logging module.
findings.gap: Library/CLI tool. Tracing is a consumer concern.

findings.title: No alerting — consumer responsibility
findings.description: Library/utility — alerting is a consumer concern. Zappa does not configure CloudWatch alarms for deployed applications.

findings.title: No business metrics — deployment tool
findings.description: Zappa is a deployment tool with no business outcomes to measure. Deployed user applications should define their own business metrics.

evaluations.reason: application repository with library N/A mapping applied (dev-library-application override)
application repository with library N/A mapping applied (dev-library-application override)
application repository with library N/A mapping applied (dev-library-application override)
application repository with library N/A mapping applied (dev-library-application override)
application repository with library N/A mapping applied (dev-library-application override)
